title: Food needs startup money
message: A real food business needs commercial kitchen access, ingredients, packaging, food safety certification and insurance — rarely under $2,000 to launch.
socratic: If a commercial kitchen rental alone can cost $500/month, and you still need ingredients, packaging and certification — where does your current budget get spent first?
socratic_hint: The student wants a food business with under $2,000. Probe the real line-item costs: commercial kitchen, ingredients, packaging, food handler certification, and liability insurance.

title: Products need materials to exist
message: Physical products need materials, manufacturing, packaging, and starter inventory — a realistic minimum is $1,000 before the first sale.
socratic: Have you priced out materials, packaging, and minimum order quantities? What's the actual cost to produce your first 20 units?
socratic_hint: Student wants to sell physical products with under $1,000. Ask them to name specific materials, packaging costs, and minimum order quantities — the real startup cost is almost always higher than expected.

title: Hard to grind on something you don't love
message: 10+ hours per week is serious commitment. Disinterest makes pushing through the hard days almost impossible.
socratic: What will you tell yourself on a bad day to keep working 10+ hours on an idea you're not excited about?
socratic_hint: Student commits 10+ hours/week but rates their excitement as 'Not really'. Challenge them to articulate a specific motivation that could sustain that workload long-term.

title: Enthusiasm gap
message: Passion makes 7 hours feel like 3 — and its absence makes 3 feel like 30.
socratic: Is there a similar idea you'd actually be excited about? What would it take to pursue that instead?
socratic_hint: Student commits 7-9 hours/week but feels low excitement. Help them explore whether a slight pivot in their idea could unlock genuine enthusiasm.

title: You love it — but do your customers?
message: Your passion doesn't guarantee others share the same problem. You need at least one real conversation before investing time or money.
socratic: What if your first potential customer says they don't actually need this — what would you do next?
socratic_hint: Student is highly passionate but hasn't validated with a single customer, and started from interest/money not a real problem. Probe the gap between internal excitement and confirmed external demand.

title: Food needs taste-testers first
message: Flavours, allergies, and preferences vary hugely. Spending money on ingredients before getting feedback is a real risk.
socratic: Who could you give a free sample to this week and get brutally honest feedback from?
socratic_hint: Student wants a food business but has talked to nobody. Ask them to identify one specific person they could give a free sample to before spending any money.

title: Travel-based biz needs independence
message: At Grade 6-8 in a rural area, every customer visit would depend on an adult driving you. That's a fragile plan.
socratic: Who would drive you to your customers every single week, and have you actually confirmed they can commit to that?
socratic_hint: Young student in a rural area chose travel-based delivery. Ask them to name the specific adult who will drive them and whether that person knows this plan.

title: Big effort with no clear 'why'
message: When things get tough — and they always do — not knowing why you started makes it easy to quit.
socratic: Think of a time you worked really hard on something. What kept you going when it got difficult?
socratic_hint: Student invests 7+ hours/week but doesn't know their own 'why'. Guide them to identify a personal motivation or past experience that could anchor their commitment.

title: No passion + no purpose = high risk
message: Two red flags at once: you're not excited AND not sure why you're doing it. This combination rarely leads to follow-through.
socratic: What would a business you actually get excited about look like? Let's start there instead.
socratic_hint: Student has both low passion and unclear purpose — the most dangerous combination. Help them identify one thing they genuinely enjoy that could become a business, before addressing any other detail.

title: Experienced seller, zero research
message: You know HOW to sell — but have you checked if people actually want this specific idea?
socratic: Your sales skills are a real advantage. Why haven't you tested this specific idea with even one person yet?
socratic_hint: An experienced seller who has not validated their specific new idea. Challenge them to apply their sales instincts to market research — they already have the skill, just not the habit.

title: Service business with no skills or experience
message: A service business with no budget and no experience is possible — but you need to explain exactly what skill you're selling. What can you do better than a peer?
socratic: What specifically would you do for a customer, and why would they choose you over someone with more experience?
socratic_hint: Student wants a service business but has no budget and no experience. Don't dismiss it — ask: what specific task or skill could you offer right now? Lawn mowing, tutoring a subject you're strong in, helping neighbours with technology?

title: Very high commitment for a junior student
message: 10+ hours per week is a very large commitment at your school stage — have you accounted for homework, sport, travel, and family time? A more realistic target is 3–6 hours.
socratic: Walk me through a typical school week. Where exactly do these 10 hours fit in without missing anything important?
socratic_hint: A Grade 6-8 student is committing 10+ hours per week. Ask them to map out a full school week — classes, homework, sport, family dinner — and find where the hours actually come from. This is usually a planning gap, not a motivation gap.

title: Online business with no outreach plan
message: An online business that has spoken to nobody yet has no idea if anyone will find their page. What platform or channel will your first 10 customers come from?
socratic: Name one specific online place — a group chat, a community forum, a social media page — where your target customer already hangs out. How would you reach them there?
socratic_hint: Student has an online delivery model but has spoken to zero potential customers. Ask them to name one specific online community or platform where their customer type is already active — then ask how they'd make their first 10 sales from there.
